headline: Greece Launches Tiny Satellites to Catch Wildfires Early
meta_description: Four nanosatellites smaller than carry-on bags now detect wildfires when they are just 13 feet across—far earlier than conventional satellites.
summary_short: Greece has deployed four nanosatellites that detect wildfires at just 4 meters wide, catching fires 100 times earlier than conventional satellites.
body:
Conventional satellite technology has a serious limitation when it comes to wildfire detection: fires must grow to roughly the size of a cruise ship before they become visible from orbit. This delay can mean the difference between a contained incident and a catastrophic blaze. Greece saw an opportunity to change this equation.

In May, the country launched four OroraTech nanosatellites into low Earth orbit. Each device is smaller than a carry-on suitcase, yet capable of detecting a wildfire when it is only about 4 meters (13 feet) across. This represents a dramatic leap in early warning capability—catching fires approximately 100 times smaller than conventional systems can detect.

The implications for fire management are substantial. Earlier detection means faster response, which can dramatically improve the chances of containing a fire before it spreads widely. For a Mediterranean region increasingly threatened by wildfires, this technological advancement offers a concrete tool for protecting communities and landscapes.
